title: Robotics closed-end fund vs MSTR premium replay
question: If a robotics closed-end fund trades at a persistent private-access premium and uses a MicroStrategy-style issuance flywheel, do early public investors benefit?
finding: Conditional. A premium can be rational when the holdings are private and scarce, so the right test is not "premium bad" but whether NAV/share compounds before the premium fades. Using published MSTR/BTC/BOT price extracts plus Strategy BTC/share data, MSTR decomposes into asset price, asset-per-share accretion and mNAV compression/expansion. BOT starts at roughly 5.1x reported NAV on the latest Massive close used here and its public SEC schedule is concentrated in Apptronik, Figure AI and Dyna, so early investors need either fast holding-level NAV/share growth, durable private-access multiple persistence, or both. Good sector, bad entry remains the main failure mode.
